title: Frames and Frame Rates (FPS)
concept: Frame rate, measured in Frames Per Second (FPS), determines how many static frames are displayed each second to create motion.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is a 'frame' in a video?

options.text: A static image within the video sequence
The border around the video player
A type of neural network layer

exercises.explain: Yes! A frame is one of the individual static images that make up a video sequence.

cards.title: Why does old video look choppy?
cards.body: Have you ever watched film footage from the early 1900s? The motion looks jerky and sped up. That is because old cameras recorded fewer frames each second than modern cameras do!

cards.title: Frames Per Second (FPS)
cards.body: Frames Per Second (FPS) is the unit used to measure frame rate. It tells us how many individual images are captured or played back in one second of video. Standard video is usually 24, 30, or 60 FPS.

cards.title: Walking steps vs. strides
cards.body: Think of FPS as steps. If you take 3 steps per second (high FPS), your walk looks smooth and continuous. If you only take 1 step every two seconds (low FPS), your movement looks disjointed and teleporting.

cards.title: Gaming vs. Cinema
cards.body: Movies are traditionally shot at 24 FPS, which gives a cinematic look with natural motion blur. Video games are often played at 60 FPS or higher to make controls feel responsive and motion look crisp.

cards.title: More isn't always better
cards.body: You might think we should always use the highest FPS possible. However, doubling the FPS from 30 to 60 doubles the data size! For an AI model, processing 60 FPS requires twice the memory and computation, often with little extra accuracy gain.

cards.title: High FPS vs. Low FPS
cards.body: Comparing the tradeoffs of different frame rates:

left.text:
Less data to process
Choppy motion
Objects may travel far between frames

right.text:
Heavy computational load
Ultra-smooth motion
Tiny object displacement between frames

exercises.prompt: What does the abbreviation 'FPS' stand for in video processing?
exercises.explain: Correct! FPS stands for Frames Per Second.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Doubling a video's frame rate doubles the number of image frames a computer must process.
exercises.explain: True. Higher FPS means more frames are packed into each second of video, increasing processing requirements.

exercises.sentence: If a video has a ___ FPS, objects will appear to 'teleport' or jump between consecutive frames.
exercises.explain: At low FPS, the time gap between frames is large, so fast-moving objects travel long distances between frames, looking choppy.

exercises.prompt: Which of these frame rates requires the MOST computation to analyze a 10-second clip?
exercises.explain: 60 FPS requires processing 600 frames, whereas 30 FPS requires 300, and 15 FPS requires 150.

exercises.prompt: You are designing a self-driving car vision system. Why might choosing 10 FPS be dangerous compared to 30 FPS?

options.text: Because the car travels several feet between frames, delaying critical braking decisions.
Because 10 FPS changes the colors of traffic lights to grayscale.
Because the camera sensor will run out of power at low rates.
Because 10 FPS prevents the model from doing any 2D convolutions.

exercises.explain: Exactly! If a car moves at highway speeds, a delay of even 1/10th of a second between frames represents many feet of travel. A high frame rate is critical for fast safety decisions. Next: let's explore spatial vs. temporal dimensions!
